title: Sets
description: Sets provide O(1) membership testing — critical for duplicate detection, intersection problems, and converting brute-force O(n) lookups into O(1) checks.

sections.title: Creating a Set
sections.explanation: Create sets with `new Set()` or `new Set(iterable)`. Sets automatically deduplicate — passing an array with duplicates gives a set of unique values. The `.size` property gives the count. This is the fastest way to get unique elements in JavaScript.

exercise.prompt: Create a Set from the array [5, 3, 5, 1, 3, 1]. Print the size, then print whether the set has 3, and whether it has 9.

validation.message: 3 unique values, has(3) is true, has(9) is false
Use Set constructor, .size, and .has() for membership testing

exercise.hint: The set deduplicates to {5, 3, 1} — size is 3. Use .has() to check membership.

sections.title: add, has, and delete
sections.explanation: add() inserts a value (ignored if already present), has() checks membership in O(1), delete() removes a value. These three operations cover most interview scenarios: tracking visited nodes, checking for duplicates, and maintaining a working set during BFS/DFS.

exercise.prompt: Create an empty Set. Add values 10, 20, 30. Print has(20). Delete 20, then print has(20) and the final size.

validation.message: has(20) is true before delete, false after, size is 2
Use add, has, and delete for set operations

exercise.hint: Add 20 and 30, then delete 20. Final set is {10, 30} with size 2.

sections.title: Set intersection
sections.explanation: To find common elements between two collections, put one in a Set and filter the other by membership. This is O(n+m) vs O(n*m) brute force. This pattern appears in problems like finding common characters, array intersection, and validating against allowed sets.

exercise.prompt: Find the intersection of arrays [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] and [3, 5, 7, 9]. Convert the first to a Set, filter the second, sort the result, and print joined by commas.

validation.message: Use Set.has inside filter for O(n) intersection

exercise.hint: Filter arrB by checking if setA.has(x) for each element.

sections.title: Array deduplication with Set
sections.explanation: The most concise dedup pattern: `[...new Set(arr)]`. This preserves first-occurrence order and runs in O(n). In interviews, you might need to deduplicate while maintaining order, or count unique elements — Set handles both cases elegantly.

exercise.prompt: Given `arr = [4, 2, 7, 2, 4, 9, 7, 1]`, deduplicate using a Set spread, print the unique count, then print the deduped array sorted ascending and joined by commas.

validation.message: Use spread with Set for deduplication

exercise.hint: Use [...new Set(arr)] to deduplicate. Sort numerically with (a, b) => a - b.
